The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

replies.title: Re: Occult Training, Spirituality
replies.content:
"Realistic" means something different to me than to most people.

There's a technique among recent-generation rationalists for adjusting which psychological forces are affecting one's imagination about the future, by simply imagining that one is making a bet about the future and trying to work out the odds at which one would switch over from wanting to take one side of the bet to wanting to take the other side.

The underlying theory is that people's ideas about the future normally exist in a haze full of "beliefs as attire" and "luxury beliefs". These beliefs might superficially resemble beliefs about details of the future, like, the sort of beliefs which by which one normally might beneficially guide one's actions, by considering how those actions would interact with the specified details of the imagined future to generate better or worse consequences. But since the beliefs are mostly about far-away consequences that, at least under evolutionary circumstances, individual action didn't meaningfully influence, they invisibly became a natural language for other things -- like signaling loyalties and prospective factional alliances, or demonstrating memetic fashion awareness and well-connectedness, or a way of focusing or testing collective-action precommitments (e.g. the houris that we hear about jihadis believing in).

(The iconoclastic economist Robin Hanson even has a theory that humans have developed an automatic scale for hedging away socially-convenient beliefs from the beliefs that actually determine what they'll do in practice. According to this theory, beliefs in the "far mode" end of the spectrum belong to this hazy otherworld where everyone's motivations and collective actions work in a perfect idealized way -- or, if enemies, a perfect idealized depraved way -- for no truly coherent reason, and everything is inflected by the same psychological tendencies that when concentrated in Western imagination give us imaginary utopian cities where everyone is energy beings wearing white togas and the buildings are all geometrically perfect crystal/silver/alabaster spires rising into blue cloudless skies. "Near mode" beliefs, when they exist against a backdrop of far-mode beliefs, belong instead to a perpetually exceptional world where the pecadillos and foibles of oneself and one's allies are always only to be expected and excused given whatever the circumstances were this time, and if you're a filmmaker trying to kick viewers out of "far mode" you make everything brown and grimy. With this scale as sort of the human baseline, it then takes extra work to actually have beliefs that would be genuinely inconvenient to honestly report about the probable future conduct of oneself or of the groups that one identifies with.)

One refinement of this betting-odds technique is to consider multiple intersecting possibilities and check whether one's betting odds on the more specific possibilities add up to one's claimed betting odds on the more general ones. Another is to actually engage in bets against other people. The forums where cultivation of "realism" according to this technique has reached the highest intensity are prediction betting markets like Manifold (using play money that converts to charitable donations) or Polymarket (using real money). While it's difficult to interpret some of the market odds because of complications like losses from leaving money parked in a bet or some of the outcomes (e.g. world war) implying difficulties receiving payouts, for short-range questions they seem remarkably well-calibrated, and in the medium future a plurality or majority of the money seems to be on ... certain outcomes discussion of which is discouraged here.

That being the case, I have three suggestions:

1. Contemplate Rudolf Steiner's visionary myth of the contingent age of spiders:

https://rsarchive.org/Lectures/GA204/English/AP1987/19210513p02.html and https://en.anthro.wiki/Spider_creatures (the following splices two variations of the lecture)

> it may appear comparatively harmless to people when they think only those automatic, lifeless thoughts that arise through comprehension of the mineral world itself and the mineral element's effects in plant, animal, and man. [...] if men were to continue thinking in this way, if men were really to develop nothing but such thoughts, up to the time when, in the 8th millennium, lunar existence will again unite with earthly existence, what would then come into being? [...] these thoughts of men, which have no reality, will become reality at one stroke when the moon unites with the earth. And out of the earth will sprout a terrible race of beings, which in their character stand between the mineral kingdom and the vegetable kingdom as automaton-like beings with a superabundant intellect, with an intense intellect. With this movement, which will take hold over the earth, the earth will be covered as with a web, a web of terrible spiders, spiders of a gigantic wisdom, which, however, in their organisation do not even reach up to the plant existence, terrible spiders, which will entangle themselves in each other, which in their outer movements will imitate everything that men thought out with the shadowy intellect [...] To the extent that human beings have not enlivened their shadowy, intellectual concepts, they will have to unite their being[...] with these ghastly mineral-plant-like spidery creatures. They will have to dwell together with these spiders; they will have to seek their further progress in cosmic evolution in the evolutionary stream that this spider brood will then assume.

2. Contemplate the myth of the three days of darkness.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three_Days_of_Darkness

Not mentioned in the wikipedia article is the part of the myth where the darkness outside will contain voices imitating distressed strangers or loved ones trying to persuade people to open their doors. (The element of "anyone who opens their window and looks out into the darkness will die" is curiously parallel to David Langford's story "Different Kinds of Darkness".)

3. Contemplate the koan "Seijo's Soul Is Separated" or "Wuzi's True Qian".

This appears in Gateless Gate as case 35, but a more evocative telling is https://uncertainty.club/qian-is-separated-from-her-soul-the-koan-story/ . The question at the tip of the koan, "which one was truly her?" is meant to create focus, but that doesn't mean it otherwise works like an ordinary question.

comments.title: American Culture?
comments.content:
Good Day, Mr. Greer,

I sometimes get an urge to insert some leaven in America’s “cultural life”. My feeling is that most countries with a long period of development have a culture that slowly evolved, sometimes from the grassroots up and sometimes (as in the monarchies of Europe) from the top down.

However, I personally don’t think that what America possesses counts as a culture which contributes to the inner (soul level) development and well-being of the populace. If I were a writer like you (you who have indeed contributed mightily to the cultural health of the world) I would write — but I am a classical musician instead, recreating European culture.

My question is, I have an idea that America as a whole needs to understand or access some sort of inner bar or standard. Even our money system has no standard, and it’s progressing more and more in an unhinged direction with the iteration of Bitcoin. But it’s an inner, spiritual standard I’m interested in; something which allows a person to choose for the welfare of himself and others. I am doubtful about the Buddhist meme, “There is no good and evil.” Especially these days.

So, I feel there needs to be an insertion of a “moral gold standard” in the popular psyche.

You’re a student of society and philosophy. Do you have any idea if there has ever been such a thing (other than religion, which people currently generally automatically opt out of), or if it would even be possible to place such a seed in the popular mind?

Thanks for your time!

replies.title: Re: American Culture?
replies.content:
The first thing to realize is that America is not yet civilized. It's a barbarian society that has adopted some of the habits of formerly dominant civilized societies, the way Visigoth kings used to parade around in togas and coin crude imitations of Roman money.

The second thing to realize is that the ripening of a culture can't be hurried. It'll take us about as long as it took the Visigoths -- say, another 500 years or so -- before an authentic American civilization begins to emerge here. Until then, all attempts to civilize us will amount to trying to impose an alien pattern on a burgeoning barbarian society, and that always fails.

The third thing to realize is that the rejection of religion in American society was never much more than a temporary fad borrowed toga-fashion from European intellectuals -- it never really sank in past the level of the comprador class of European-influenced intellectuals -- and the weakness of current religious institutions is another sign of European cultural imports breaking down. Out there among the living-room churches, the strip-mall tabernacles, the trailer-trash Wicca covens, the redneck Heathens who use shotguns instead of swords to trace the boundaries of their sacred space, and the rest of it, a genuine religious ferment is under way. Over the centuries ahead, I expect it to give rise to a distinctly American religious sensibility, which will become the seed from which the future American civilization will rise.

Thus you're correct in pointing to what's needed, but incorrect in thinking that it can be provided artificially. These things develop organically, in their own time, and the current stage of barbarism here in America will go through the usual changes and give rise to the usual results no matter what you or anybody else in the intelligentsia does. I know that's a bitter pill to swallow, but that can't be helped.

replies.title: Re: American Culture?
replies.content:
1) Good question, but I've met quite a few of both.

2) Fortune's a good example of what happens when someone sets out consciously to seed something into the collective consciousness. Through her books and the Rites of Isis and Pan, she set out to transform the English-speaking world's attitude toward sex away from Victorian prudery. She succeeded in banishing Victorian prudery for a while, only to replace it with Roman debauchery -- and now the pendulum is swinging back toward prudery.

I'd suggest, in fact, that her work was an effect rather than a cause. The English-speaking world goes through roughly century-long cycles of one or the other dysfunctional attitude toward sex -- debauched in the Elizabethan era, prudish in the Puritan era that followed, debauched in the Georgian era, prudish in the Victorian era, debauched in the 20th century and beginning to veer back toward prudery in our time. Fortune caught, or was caught by, the wave of her time. All things considered, how much good did it do?

replies.title: Re: American Culture?
replies.content:
1.) I have met some of the types as well, Wiccan trailer-trash especially, and seen evidence of the latter online and in print. Maybe now they are becoming post-Wiccan trailer trash.

2). Good point. I see Crowley as contributing majorly to the libertine attitudes of the debauched 20th century. I suppose both he and Fortune could have been caught by the wave, each in their own way.

How much good did it do? I suppose limited good for a time, until it pushed past the middle ground and into the other side of things.

Your point brings home the notion that the mage is better off changing themselves than trying to change the world. There often unintended consequences in the latter, and mages are human too, and though may be see farther than some, still have limited view of the future, and little control once the magic leaves the gate.

3.) Q: Accepting both freedom to choose, and the limits imposed on people by fate, how much is a mage and writer like Fortune or Crowley, also manifesting their destiny, and setting these things into motion because the inner plane contacts and forces within the currents they are working are also pushing things that way? Like in the instance that an artist is a vessel. Things come to them unbidden. Characters, as you know, take on a life of their own. I guess I can see it being easy to get caught up in currents, or be pushed certain ways by various forces. Sometimes cooperation might look like inspiration to a writer or artist/mage.

Of course there is also just the age old human problem of thinking we know best.

I don't know if this is relevant, but: There was a singular video game "Clair Obscur: Expedition 33" released by a French studio in late April, with a few odd synchronicities and providences associated with its development. It's set in a fantasy variation of the Belle Époque, with an overwhelming profusion of artistic richness in the visuals and music. The plot is a tragic conflict over destroying the last of something otherwise already gone to enforce waking up to a bereaved but still-livable reality, from a dream of reminiscence that had needlessly become lethally addictive. It made a huge impact on the audience, being expected to make Game of the Year, and by one measure on a prominent review aggregator the highest-rated game of all time, despite its being the first release from a new small-ish studio on a modest budget. Some of the leads of the studio didn't even have prior experience in the roles they were working in, and another was an undiscovered perhaps industry-world-class composer only recruited by a chance connection that might have been missed with a few hours' difference of one-time visits to an obscure forum.

It is among other things a dramatic presentation of a mix of historical French artistic influences and cosmopolitan influences interpreted by a French artistic sensibility. The last time distinctively artistically-French media had something like this big of an impact on global audiences was probably Amélie. But this time it feels a little more like a purposeful display, made by something that knew that it was fading or changing, to try to convey what kind of vision or realizability had once existed -- as the game says, "for the ones who come after".

replies.content:
Along the lines of "what you resist persists" a basic meditative practice I have come across is when a distracting thought comes to mind to interrupt meditation, one should simply hold it in mind, acknowledge it exists, and then let go of it without making any more fuss about it than necessary. If it pops up again, repeat the process. Eventually, whatever pattern that is holding the thought in place will have run its course and the song won't bother you anymore.

You may also wish to pay attention for whether there are times when the song is more or less intrusive than others. You may be focusing on the song instead of attending to some other matter. Naturally, making an effort to attend to whatever matter you are avoiding may also send the song on its way as it would then have nothing to distract you from.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Have you tried simply listening to the song again? Play it loud, on the best sound system you have access to. Pay close attention to every instrument, note, and word, as if it holds the secret to life itself. You might do this more than once through in quick succession (but once might be enough).

This works for me most of the time. (But I'm not as rich as Elon Musk, so...) My theory is, when you have a song stuck in your head, your mind is repeating it to yourself from memory because there's something about the actual song you haven't heard yet at some level.

In Germanic folklore it’s often said that excessive grieving by the living weighs down the souls of the dead. That message is especially pointed at parents grieving their lost child, and is shown in images of small, deceased children lugging heavy jugs of tears that their parents have cried, or wandering around in dripping wet clothing.

comments.title: Music, western tonality
comments.content:
This came to my notice recently: https://mymodernmet.com/neanderthal-flute-sounds/
(The video embeded in the article has a sound track of playing the recreation, well worth listening to even if you are not a video watcher-I normally am not but managed to watch for the clips of playing technique.)

While musicians are all delighted, as indeed we should be, it immediately came to my mind that this anchors the Western 7 note scale MUCH further back in time than anyone had anticipated, assuming the recreation is accurate and that seems like a trivially easy thing to check (thickness and spacing) so there's a low chance of a hoax.

The musician involved, Ljuben Dimkaroski, plays such famous tunes as Beethoven's Ode to Joy on it. Looking at the instrument, it looks much more like a modern recorder than a modern flute, and he plays, as best I can tell, like a cross between a recorder and a cornet.
If the dating is accurate, we Westerners have been committed to our seven note scale pattern for some 43,000 years.
Seems to me that those of us involved in music are working with something much larger and deeper than we had guessed, and while we know music is powerful magic, and can provoke great response in the listeners as well as performers, we perhaps need to sit down and analyze our compositions with this depth of time in mind.
If my fellow Ecosophians have never considered that the French pick La Marsellaise as their national song, the Brits pick God Save the King, and Americans picked the jovial drinking tune To Anacreon in Heaven; and the national tendencies of those countries, I urge you to listen to the three tunes and consider the history of those polities. Not the words, the tunes.
And now, I shall go practice.
Thank you, Brother Greer, as always, for hosting Magic Monday.

BoysMom

replies.title: Re: Music, western tonality
replies.content: Interesting. And yet we know for a fact that the ancient Greeks used modality rather than tonality, as did the European Middle Ages -- the standard major scale aka Ionian mode survived only as a folk-song scale during that latter period. So clearly it's more that the standard seven note scale is one option with a very long (pre)history...

replies.title: Re: Music, western tonality
replies.content:
The traditional seven modes-Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian, all use the same pattern, just start and end on a different pitch in the pattern.
That's actually how I teach my students the patterns: play a scale in the key signature of C starting on the D, that's Dorian. Personally I'm partial to Phrygian and Mixolydian out of the set.

The other possible seven pitches between the octaves patterns, say, setting the two half-steps next to each other, don't occur often. Whole tone-only six notes between the octaves-seems more of a modern thing. But it's not so far back in time before Western music was transmitted musician to musician and not written, and these preserved instruments can tell us only so much.
That this ancient instrument plays half steps and whole steps that are recognizably the same intervals we use . . . that's the thing. There's no physical reason they couldn't have divided, say, into an even ten intervals and picked six pitches, instead of the twelve we use and then pick seven pitches to make scales.

BoysMom

replies.title: Re: Music, western tonality
replies.content: East Asian and Native American music is pretty consistently pentatonic rather than heptatonic -- Japanese gagaku music, interestingly enough, has a full set of pentatonic modes -- and ancient Greek music is much closer to Indian music than medieval-to-modern European music. I'm left to wonder if the heptatonic system is specifically a Neanderthal inheritance, as it seems to be native only in societies with a noticeable Neanderthal ancestry.

replies.title: Re: Music, western tonality
replies.content:
This stuff is super cool!

On the clip you posted, there's a ton of sliding, bending, and adjusting. I suspect that the main factor is a trumpet player's amazingly flexible vocal tract and great ears, rather than the physics of the instrument. He's playing what he hears, which is largely based on his familiarity with the major scale.

Wikipedia has a short clip of the same guy pretty closely approximating the F, G, and Ab major scales!
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divje_Babe_flute

Reminds me of the story of a certain saxophone guru who used to play a descending major scale while fingering the notes of an ascending chromatic scale... "it's all mental," he used to tell his students.

Noodles

replies.title: Re: Hero Myths
replies.content:
Hmm. My immediate concern is that most hero myths involve epic amounts of raw violence and murder, and the Irish examples are pretty intense even by hero myth standards. How many people died horribly because Ailill and Medb had that conversation in bed?

Sure, you can say "Okay, I'll approach that symbolically," but once you do that you're not using the myth as a framework, you're using whatever symbolic structure you read into the myth as your framework, and that's usually some subconscious muddling together of personal history and themes from pop culture. Lacking a well-understood philosophical or theological framework, you'll almost always just rehash your own current problems under new labels.

Years ago we had a dog about 8 months old who normally showed not the slightest interest in the stuffed toy we’d bought for him. The dog came to sit with us in our temple, but when we started drumming to call our spirit-animal companions he got up and left. We figured he didn’t like the drumming and went on with the ceremony.

A few minutes later pup came back upstairs to the temple with his stuffed bear and lay down alertly in sphinx position, holding it between his paws. Apparently he felt he needed his own spirit-animal companion! He stayed that way with us for the rest of the ceremony, alert and unmoving. That was the only time he had paid any attention to the stuffie.

We loved his participation! When we do this ceremony in a group we can usually sense or catch sight of each other from time to time during the journey, and sure enough I could see him in the distance, off on his own affairs. He was a very primitive looking, wolfy dog, rangy muscled, dark-brindled with a thick ruff, and always with a very intense, alert demeanor. Really, just the kind of dog you might expect to meet on a shamanic journey! The glimpses of him lurking in the Otherworldly woods added greatly to our experiences.

comments.title: The Four Elements, or Five
comments.content:
Traditionally, there were four, or sometimes five elements: Earth, Water, Air, Fire, and sometimes Aether. I gather that there are elementals that correspond to the first four, but are there Aether elementals? And what exactly is Aether? -- is that rare-ified stuff material (ie plasma), magical, or in-between?
Also, these elements are represented as triangles, either pointing up or down, and with a line across or without (I'm sure you know what I mean). How did those symbols originate, and are they magically charged?
This conception of the elements is found across the traditions of various cultures, and seems to be very ancient, possibly dating back to Atlantean times, although that's pure speculation on my part. I wonder if you can elaborate? As always, I'm interested in all relevant info.

replies.title: Re: The Four Elements, or Five
replies.content:
The number of elements varies from culture to culture; there's some old Welsh material that has a three-element system, for example. I see all this as a matter of culturally derived human categories rather than objective realities. The five-element system with aether descends from India, where the fifth element is akasha, space -- there's also the very different Chinese five-element system of fire, wind/wood, metal, water, and earth.

What is aether? The word's originally Greek, and it was used by some theorists for the material substance that they thought filled what we call "outer space" -- in ancient times it was commonly believed that an actual vacuum was impossible. When you see discussions of aether in medieval and Renaissance texts, that's what they're talking about: a substance clearer than glass but stronger than steel, the raw material of which the celestial spheres were made. Later on, after the concept of vacuum became scientifically acceptable, the word got repurposed for the life force.

As for those symbols, I don't happen to know where they come from, but they were current in Renaissance alchemical literature. Are they magically charged? In my experience, weakly -- that's one of the reasons I adopted the different symbols I learned from John Gilbert.

replies.content:

I'm aware of other systems, like the Chinese as you mentioned, or the trigrams of the Book of Changes with the in-between elements of Marsh, Mountain and Lightning, but I was trying to stay concise.

It seems aether was originally like the "firmament" that fundamentalist Christians still believe in, an artifact of a time when the material world was not as well understood.
